text: BFS Pattern: Shortest path in unweighted graph. Use when all edges have equal weight and you want minimum hops. Template: queue=deque([start]), visited={start}, while queue: node=queue.popleft(), for neighbor in graph[node]: if neighbor not in visited: visited.add(neighbor); queue.append(neighbor). Time: O(V+E), Space: O(V).

BFS Pattern: Level-order traversal on binary tree. Process nodes level by level. Template: queue=deque([root]), while queue: level_size=len(queue), for _ in range(level_size): node=queue.popleft(), process(node), if node.left: queue.append(node.left), if node.right: queue.append(node.right). Time: O(n), Space: O(n) for wide trees.

BFS Pattern: Multi-source BFS. Start BFS from multiple sources simultaneously. Use when finding minimum distance from any of several sources. Initialize queue with all sources and mark all as visited. Example: rotting oranges, walls and gates, 0-1 matrix. Avoids running BFS from each source separately. Time: O(V+E).

BFS Pattern: 0-1 BFS using deque. When edges have weight 0 or 1, use deque instead of queue. Weight-0 edge: appendleft (prepend), weight-1 edge: append. Acts like Dijkstra but O(V+E) instead of O((V+E)logV). Use for grid problems with varying move costs. dist initialized to infinity, updated when shorter path found.

BFS Pattern: Word ladder / state transformation. Use BFS when states can be transformed one step at a time and you need minimum steps. Represent each state as a node. Generate all valid next states, avoid revisited states. Example: word ladder changes one letter at a time. State space can be huge; use bidirectional BFS for optimization. Time: O(26^L * L * N).

BFS Pattern: Grid BFS (island/flood fill). Traverse a 2D grid treating each cell as a node. 4-directional or 8-directional movement. Mark visited by modifying grid or using visited set. Use for: number of islands, max area of island, flood fill. Template: for each unvisited land cell, BFS to explore entire connected component. Time: O(m*n).

BFS Pattern: Topological sort (Kahn's algorithm). BFS-based approach for DAG ordering. Compute in-degrees. Add all 0-in-degree nodes to queue. Pop node, reduce in-degree of neighbors; add to queue if in-degree becomes 0. Result is topological order. Detect cycle if not all nodes processed. Time: O(V+E).

BFS Pattern: Bidirectional BFS. Run BFS from both source and target simultaneously. Meet in the middle to reduce search space from O(b^d) to O(b^(d/2)) where b=branching factor, d=depth. Use when source and target are both known. Check intersection of frontiers each level. Effective for word ladder, six degrees of separation. Time: O(b^(d/2)).

BFS Pattern: Minimum steps to reach target with constraints. When movement is constrained (knight moves, specific jumps), model as graph. Each position is a node, valid moves are edges. BFS gives minimum moves. Example: minimum knight moves on chessboard. Can optimize with bidirectional BFS or A*. Time: O(V+E) where V is reachable states.

BFS Pattern: Cycle detection in undirected graph. BFS finds cycle if a visited neighbor is encountered that is not the parent. Track parent for each node. If neighbor is visited and is not parent of current node, cycle exists. Works for disconnected graphs by iterating over all unvisited nodes. Time: O(V+E).

BFS Pattern: Connected components count. Iterate over all nodes; for each unvisited node, run BFS to mark entire component. Increment count per BFS call. Use for: number of islands, friend circles, number of provinces. Works on both graph and grid representations. Time: O(V+E) or O(m*n) for grids.

BFS Pattern: Shortest path with state (e.g., keys, obstacles). Augment BFS state with additional info: (row, col, keys_collected), (node, fuel_remaining). Use tuple as state for visited set. Explosion of state space requires careful design. Example: shortest path with K obstacles eliminated, collect keys and open locks. Time: O(V * 2^K) with K keys.

BFS Pattern: BFS on implicit graph (sliding puzzle, rubik's cube). States are game configurations. Edges are valid moves. BFS finds optimal solution. Represent state as tuple/string for hashing. Example: 8-puzzle, sliding puzzle. Time exponential in state space size but BFS still finds shortest solution. Bidirectional BFS reduces exploration.

BFS Pattern: Rotten oranges / time-based BFS. Multi-source BFS where each step represents one unit of time. Track time as BFS level. Count fresh items; when they all become rotten, return time elapsed. If fresh items remain after BFS, return -1. Pattern: BFS naturally tracks 'minimum time to infect/spread'. Time: O(m*n).

BFS Pattern: Walls and gates. Fill each empty room with distance to nearest gate. Multi-source BFS starting from all gates simultaneously. Spreads distance outward; each cell gets the minimum distance automatically because BFS visits cells in order of distance. Time: O(m*n). Pattern is push-based (from sources) rather than pull-based (from each cell).

BFS Pattern: Bipartite graph check. BFS colors graph with 2 colors. Start with color 0. Assign alternating colors to neighbors. If a neighbor already has the same color, graph is not bipartite. Works for disconnected graphs by checking each component. Used in: detect if graph can be 2-colored, divide into 2 groups with no internal edges. Time: O(V+E).

BFS Pattern: Shortest path in weighted graph (Dijkstra). Use priority queue (min-heap) instead of plain queue. Pop (dist, node), skip if dist > known dist[node]. For each neighbor, relax edge: if dist[node]+weight < dist[neighbor], push new state. Time: O((V+E) log V). Use for: network delay, cheapest flights within K stops (modified).

BFS Pattern: Clone graph. BFS with a hash map from original node to clone. Initialize with source clone. For each node, clone all unvisited neighbors and add to queue. If neighbor already cloned, just add edge. Preserves graph structure including cycles. Time: O(V+E), Space: O(V).

BFS Pattern: Jump game with BFS. Model positions as nodes, reachable positions as edges. BFS finds minimum jumps. Each level of BFS = one jump. More efficient: greedy tracks max_reach and next_max_reach per jump. BFS approach O(n^2), greedy O(n). Use BFS when jump destinations are complex (not just i+nums[i]).

BFS Pattern: Bus routes / group-based BFS. When you travel entire routes at once, model routes as nodes and stops as edges between routes. BFS on routes graph. Example: bus routes problem — start with all routes containing source stop, BFS to find minimum route changes to reach target. Time: O(R*S) where R=routes, S=stops per route.
